result:
Sometimes the most honest conversations happen around the edges of things we’re afraid to name. For me, right now, that unnamed thing is stillness. I grew up chasing motion, collecting achievements like they were passport stamps proving I’d been somewhere. Quiet felt dangerous, like a page I hadn’t annotated yet, or worse, the sound of someone forgetting me. But stillness has begun stalking me the way twilight overruns a room—no announcement, just gradual absorption of color. I notice it when I stop scrolling and the buzz in my body keeps scrolling for me. I notice it in the gap between questions I won’t ask the people I love most.  

I wonder if courage isn’t loud at all, but a kind of deliberate inaction: not filling the silence with opinions, letting the sunken places in myself stay sunk until they surface on their own. Trees seem fine with this arrangement; they broadcast shade without requiring applause, returning every winter to a vocabulary of bare branches. Their patience looks wasteful to the chronically ambitious, but maybe it’s the original recycling program: everything useful allowed to rot and be born again as the hush that cradles a sparrow’s first attempt at flight.  

I’m experimenting with keeping one minute each morning completely undirected. No phone, no mantra, no plan to monetize insight. Just sixty unauthorized seconds. They feel stolen from a busier deity, and sometimes they feel like cheating death. Mostly they feel like rehearsal for becoming the kind of ancestor I needed—one who knew how to sit beside the young, match their breathing, and say nothing while meaning everything.

message.content:
Sometimes the most honest conversations happen around the edges of things we’re afraid to name. For me, right now, that unnamed thing is stillness. I grew up chasing motion, collecting achievements like they were passport stamps proving I’d been somewhere. Quiet felt dangerous, like a page I hadn’t annotated yet, or worse, the sound of someone forgetting me. But stillness has begun stalking me the way twilight overruns a room—no announcement, just gradual absorption of color. I notice it when I stop scrolling and the buzz in my body keeps scrolling for me. I notice it in the gap between questions I won’t ask the people I love most.  

I wonder if courage isn’t loud at all, but a kind of deliberate inaction: not filling the silence with opinions, letting the sunken places in myself stay sunk until they surface on their own. Trees seem fine with this arrangement; they broadcast shade without requiring applause, returning every winter to a vocabulary of bare branches. Their patience looks wasteful to the chronically ambitious, but maybe it’s the original recycling program: everything useful allowed to rot and be born again as the hush that cradles a sparrow’s first attempt at flight.  

I’m experimenting with keeping one minute each morning completely undirected. No phone, no mantra, no plan to monetize insight. Just sixty unauthorized seconds. They feel stolen from a busier deity, and sometimes they feel like cheating death. Mostly they feel like rehearsal for becoming the kind of ancestor I needed—one who knew how to sit beside the young, match their breathing, and say nothing while meaning everything.
